blocks.content: Davenport came up to the porch. He didn't sit. He stood with his arms at his sides and his hat still on and looked at Julian the way he looked at things he was trying to decide how much weight to give.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: I was walking the beach around eleven. Friday night. There was a bonfire — six people, the group next door. I wasn't watching them. I was passing. But I notice things.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: The way people position themselves relative to each other. What they're looking at. What they're not looking at.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: What did you see in those six people.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: Five of them were watching the fire or each other. The way people do at a bonfire. Easy, unguarded. The way you sit with people you know.
Julian turned his mug in both hands.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: The sixth was watching one specific person. Not the fire. Not the water. One person. Consistently. With the kind of attention that isn't conversation and isn't warmth. The kind that's — calculating is a strong word. Patient is more accurate.
Davenport was quiet for a moment.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: And you're telling me this now because someone died in that house tonight.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: I'm telling you because I noticed it last night, spent tonight trying to convince myself it was nothing, and then watched you arrive at one in the morning.
